Welcome to Getting Started, Lessons with the Masters. Brad Peterson is a leading freelancer who's written winning promotions for everything from financial newsletters to pool cues. His knowledge of direct response history is virtually unmatched in the industry. For the first time ever, Brad reveals the most powerful lessons he's ever discovered, helping you to avoid many of the biggest pitfalls in advertising. Brad had so much to share, it took two CDs to fit it all in. Now, let's listen to part one. First, Brad, even before this, before talking about how you got into copyright, tell me about your childhood, your family life, and your education experience. Okay, well let's see, my childhood, you know, I grew up in a sort of, I guess, middle-class family and we always did okay, had our ups and downs. My dad was always pursuing business ventures, most of which didn't do too well, so we experienced a lot of ups and downs in that regard. So I saw firsthand, you know, what that was like to experience success and then failure and then success, and he ultimately gave up the entrepreneurial game just to get back into the corporate world, you know, because he had to achieve the kind of success he wanted to. And what kind of entrepreneurial things was your dad involved in? His field was insurance, so once the big thing I remember, the big catastrophe was him and a buddy had started an insurance agency that went bust and he wound up in some pretty major debt at the time that really hurt us. And I don't know the circumstances of the failure, I was only eight or nine years old and it all went down. Hang on, just a second. Okay, so tell me about, and all of this was in Southern California? Yeah, I grew up in San Bernardino and then we moved to San Clemente, which is in Orange County, the home of the Western White House for Richard Nixon, and so that's why I grew up in that I left San Clemente when I went to college and never moved back there, although I still, all these years later, consider it my home still, even though I've lived here and where I live now in Torrance, California, I lived here far longer than I ever lived in San Clemente, but it's a beautiful town. But I went to regular elementary high school down there in San Clemente, then I went to Loyola Marymount University for college. I was a history major and I was always a writer. I thought I'll be a writer, I'll be a journalist, I'll write books, I'll do something. Unfortunately, I never really knew about advertising or about direct response or any of that, so I never really thought about it during college. So I went to college, got my degree in history, got out, and my first real job was working for Robert Ringer, the author of Looking Out for Number One and a few other best-selling books, and I worked for him for a short time. And got, sort of got the direct response bug because everything he did was long copy, long sales copy, he was selling a financial newsletter at the time called The Tortoise Report. And so I worked for him just as a researcher as an administrative stuff. Was this before Blanchard began publishing The Tortoise Report? Yes, I lost my job when Blanchard bought The Tortoise Report. I thought about that. They moved the whole operation to New Orleans or somewhere in Louisiana and I was out. I remember Robert Ringer being on The Tonight Show, that was my first exposure to him when he was on the book tour for Looking Out for Number One. And his whole rationale was something I think that would be extremely helpful for copywriters to read. Oh, absolutely. Business people as well, the whole concept of rational selfishness. Well, you know, equally as important, I think, is winning through intimidation. This is his first book, which despite the title, which apparently offends some people, I think it's kind of a fun title, is really about not letting yourself be intimidated by your circumstances, by other people, by people opposing his experts, but essentially by people who want to keep you in your place. It was a brilliant title because you're right, the book was about winning in spite of intimidation. Right. And so, I think he's been doing this for a long time, and he's been doing this for a long time because of, but using the word through gave it an edge that really made people intrigued. Yeah, yeah. And you know, he's since updated and revised the book and came out just in the last couple of years and he's got a new version called To Be Intimidated or Not To Be Intimidated, by, you know, all his critics. And I think he tried to soften himself a little bit, but I don't think that's a real good marketing technique. No. Although he's a brilliant marketer. I mean, he knows what he's doing. I'm not... Robert Wither was... I don't... Was he working with Dan Rosenthal when he did those great full-page newspaper ads for winning through intimidation and looking up... I don't believe so. I think that was all him. I don't, you know, I don't know for sure. Because Ringer was one of the first people to develop the idea of segmenting the nation's newspapers into A, B, and C groups based on responsiveness and creating a real marketing machine for his books using full-page newspaper ads. I know both Jay Abraham, Dan Rosenthal, and myself followed on that success and built major companies using Ringer's principles. Yeah. Yeah, and it was phenomenal. I mean, he did that with his own books. He did it with Doug Casey's Crisis Investing and a few others I know. I'm trying to think of the one there was one called Nice Girls Do that was a huge best seller, I think by Irene Cusola. And he did it all with full-page ads in the Wall Street Journal and other places that drove people to bookstores. I think he sold them through the mail as well, at least in the early days, and, you know, drove his books to the top of the best seller list. One of the concepts he introduced that I used with wonderful success at Security Rare Coinup in Minneapolis was that very idea. He would have a book. He would write a full-page ad for that book. Then he would go to B Dalton and other national bookselling chains. And he would ask them to subsidize the cost of running the book in return for putting their logo in the ad along with his response device. And so he would then, in the ad, he would then say, you know, pick up this book now, your local Barnes & Noble bookseller. And he would even include a telephone number and an address at the local stores. And then, but he'd also have a response device of some kind. And it was brilliant because all a retailer like Barnes & Noble wants is store traffic. And if you can show that you're generating a response with your ad and, you know, people are reading it and responding to it through the mail, then that tells Barnes & Noble you're getting eyeballs and a lot of people will come to their store to look for it and end up buying that plus other things. So it was really wonderful. We did that at Security Rare Coin with a book. It was a 2027 forecast for 1983, I think it was. So hang on. That's wonderful. I didn't even know you worked for Robert Ringer. Oh, yeah. It was a fun experience. And I sort of caught the bug. And I'd known a little bit about direct response because of my dad's interest in mail order. But I didn't really know how it worked. And unfortunately, I didn't stay with direct response. I actually went into sales for the next three years, which was, as it's pros and cons, I wasn't the best salesman in the world, but I sure learned of the principles of selling, which translates so well into copywriting. And it made the idea of salesmanship in print much more natural for me. I saw how it translated. I was selling office equipment, copiers and typewriters, and I didn't do it by trying to entertain my clients. I did it by selling them on the benefits of the product. And when I got into advertising then, and I started reading Jay Abraham and Gary Halbert and all the early gurus, and it all made such sense because of my selling experience. So when I got into copywriting, it was an easy way to make my transition easier than it may be for some people who just don't understand sales when they get started. So you met John Finn when selling office equipment? Yes, I was out making cold calls, and I just happened to cold call his office one day. And the interesting thing actually is I cold called Jay Abraham's office, and I recognized his name and talked to somebody there, and just minutes later I cold called John Finn's office. They were in the same little office, not office park, but an area where businesses are located. And I just, and I'm looking at John and we were talking, and I mentioned that I was in Jay Abraham's office and it all came together, and John started telling me all his, you know, he knew Robert Ringer was and Doug Casey and all these direct response people. And that's when I first heard about this thing called advertising copywriting, you know, that really people did it for a living. You know, I saw Robert Ringer do it, but he had his own business. He wasn't a freelancer, and I didn't know such a thing existed. And so then it took me a while, but I just developed the idea from that, that gosh, I could do this. I've always been a writer. I can write copy. And this was in the real early days of the, you know, financial newsletters when they were just starting to boom. And I'd been interested in that topic as well. You know, I'd read Howard Ruff's books and Doug Casey's books, and so it was a good fit for me. So what year was it? Well, let's see. This is somewhere around 1985, 1986. Oh, okay. I think. And then I didn't really, you know, it took me a while to make the leap. You know, I had a job, and, but I was interested in it and talked to John a lot about it. And finally, I just couldn't, I had to get out of the selling game, so I took, just took a job at the first advertising agency I could find working in the mailroom at Foot Cone and Building, which is the agency that succeeded Lord and Thomas, which was Albert Lasker and Claude Hopkins' agency. And unfortunately, nobody at the agency even knew who Claude Hopkins was. Or cared. Or cared. And all the great principles they both had taught had longed and forgot. But I did find some. I mean, I actually found some great stuff in the archives down in the basement, old ads of theirs and stuff, and copies of some of John Kennedy's, the guy who coined the term salesmanship in print, copies of his pamphlets and books and stuff. So I had some good experiences there, and working in the mailroom was absolutely the funnest job I've ever had. Although, unfortunately, it just doesn't pay the bills. So I worked there for a while and got a call from a buddy of mine who actually was working for Jay Abraham and needed somebody to come work for Jay. So I moved over and worked for Jay. I was an editor and I did various tasks, but I edited a lot of Jay's transcripts and other things. And you know, I was, Gary Walterscheidt was the guy that worked for Jay and hired me. I was there, right? Yeah. Gary was Jay's right-hand man for quite some time. And then we both moved on. I wound up working at a local, actually another one a year, a former employer of yours, created a direct marketing group, Craig Huey. Yeah. Actually, I think it was called InfoMat when you were there, but he started an ad agency division called Creative Direct Marketing Group. And I went on there as a junior copywriter, making about $10 an hour, as I recall. Whatever copywriters were working there at the time? You know, it was only me and Kent Comi. Kent Comi was the copy chief for the Creative Director. And it was just, as I recall, just the two of us. You know, for the people who are reading this, the history of InfoMat for me was that when I left the motion picture business in 74, I answered an ad in the paper that was placed by a small mail list brokerage in Palace Verde's. And they were looking to hire a copywriter to create a creative division in the company so they could do copy and graphics as well as just rent mailing lists. And the rationale at the time was that people will rent more lists from us if they have better copy to mail. And so that's the company that I auditioned for and received a job offer the day after I submitted my sample. And it was really where I began in copywriting. And Mike Engler, who was the owner's roommate at the time, is the person who turned me on to all of the great works by John Capels, Ogilvy, Rosser Reeves, and the rest. And so that was really my entree to copywriting. I didn't begin as a freelancer. I really feel that the time I spent there was foundational. I didn't make much money. My salary was $15,000 a year. But looking back, I should have probably paid the owner for the experience I got there. But we built the creative division. We created the creative division and built it up to a million dollars in sales prior to my leaving the company. And then in the years later, Brad, as I understand, Jim Rutz followed me there. Kent Kome, you. Are there any others? Nobody. I know Gary Walterscheidt worked there at one time. But I don't know of any other. You know, anyone else was before or after my time. Yeah. And I really am not familiar with who was there after Kent left. I think I was in 77 or 78. Yeah, I left there like seven or eight years before you were there. Okay. Yeah, I started there in like 88, I believe. And then when my son was born in 1989 is when I left, soon after that, because my wife was working full time and we wanted to have somebody home with the baby. So I decided, okay, I'm just going to make the leap and become a freelancer. And then, you know, like John Finn got me some jobs and I walked into some other jobs and met people and, you know, kept managed to keep a few clients that liked my work. And, you know, just built a freelance career. And then finally, you know, I encountered you through John Finn, which gave my career a huge boost. And you know, so that's why I stand now, just keeping at it. Well, you know, it sounds like you had a lot of really great early influences. Lucking into relationships with Jay and Walterscheidt early on was incredible. Oh, yeah. Going to work for FCB. Yeah, and Gary Halbert as well had a huge influence on me. And because I went to some of his seminars and just knew him through John Finn and a lot of it comes down to John Finn. Yeah. And because he introduced me to Gary Walterscheidt, he introduced me to, well, I'm not supposed to Jay, Gary really was Jay, but he introduced me to Gary Halbert and the Robert Reiner thing I just lucked into by answering an ad in Reason Magazine. And you said a full-page ad looking for employees and I answered it and I got the job, which was kind of fun. So in addition to the wonderful personal contacts that you just, listen, there are probably three or four thousand readers of the total package who would give their right arm to wander into a relationship with people like Jay Bram and these other people. And yet it was just plain dumb luck for you. It just happened. The other thing too though is in addition to those influences, you also had face-to-face sales experience, which I think is, at AWAI I told a lot of people, you know, get a night job as a salesperson. Right. You want to be a great copywriter. You got to be a salesperson first. Copywriting is about writing. It's about sales. And yet you came to the industry with that kind of face-to-face, grind it out, overcome objections, all-fashioned salesmanship. Right, right, which really is not the best way to do it these days, but that's the way we did it then. You know, if I'd known about lead generation letters and, you know, back-end sales and keeping it on top of your past customers and all that stuff, it would have been phenomenal. But I was just, you know, had no real training. It was just, see the people, see the people, see the people. And you learn too. One thing I think you learn in sales also in addition to the principles is how hard it is to make a sale. I think a lot of people just don't really understand that it's hard to get people to part with their money. It's not just make them an offer and they'll give you their money. And there's real competition for the attention, the time, the money, and you realize you've really got to make a case. It's not just going out there and saying the name of your company, the name of your product and saying give me your money, which was what I, you know, sort of what Claude Hopkins critiqued this back in the 20s. He said, most advertisers say give me your business instead of giving it to somebody else. And that was the extent of their argument. And that just isn't good enough, especially today when everyone's so skeptical. So being in sales gives you that perspective. You see that, you know, people, and you get knocked down every day. Now I'm buying from somebody else. Now I'm buying from somebody else. And so you realize that you've got to make a case. You've got to prove it. You've got to have evidence. All the great principles, you know, you've been teaching in a total package. Well, tell me the other ways that actually having to, you know, buy groceries based on how well you sold. How did you apply those lessons effectively and hang on? How did you apply those lessons effectively when you began writing copy? You know, I think it was just knowing that I had to make a case and that I had to present benefits. I had to actually show these people here's how I am going to help you and make your life better and trying to connect with, you know, their emotional needs and wants. A lot of times you've heard this a million times, the way to get rich is to give people what they need when in reality it's give people what they want. Giving people what they need doesn't really make you rich. Giving them what they want is what makes you rich. And you go out to a business and yeah, every business needs a copy machine. But that's not good enough. They also have certain wants. I mean, they want it to be cheaper. They want the job to be done. They want it fast. They want it easy. They want the productivity increased. And, you know, so just kind of trying to focus more on the fact that I had to connect with what they actually wanted, not just on the product I was selling. You know, I made the product to hero and it was the thing that provided what they wanted. But, you know, it was focusing on the benefits and the emotional needs. Is that clear? Absolutely clear. And I think it's a wonderful insight into copywriting. I just did an article for the TILA package where I was talking about the fact that a lot of writers I work with wouldn't know a benefit if they bit them on the butt. They're not selling copy machines. Nobody wants a copy machine. Nobody wants copies. Right, yes. Yeah, exactly. That's kind of my point, yeah. People want reliability. People want to look like a hero in the bus because they saved them money. Those are the things that people want. Nobody wants a copy machine. Right, yeah. You're selling. I mean, everyone sold machines on. We make 50 copies a minute. Well, you've got to translate into something. That into something that's worthwhile. You know, what does that mean to the guy? It means that when he goes to the copy machine, he's going to be there for 30 seconds and he's going to get the jump faster, be more productive, get more done, look better in the eyes of his boss. Yeah, and the boss, it means he's going to save X number of employee hours per year. And that's more reliable to him. So by asking to make it at the time of $10,000 purchase, say, you could show him where he's going to save $50,000 a year. Either by college of hard knocks or instinctively, long before you met Abraham or read capels, you had figured out the importance of selling benefits over features. Oh, absolutely. Well, a lot of the stuff, I mean, that's why whenever someone asks me about copywriting, I always tell them to read sales books. You know, read Tom Hopkins, read Zig Ziglar. You know, find some of the, you know, classics, you know, I can't even think of the five great rules of selling, I think it's called that Percy Whiting. You know, there's so many sales books that are such a rich source of valuable and sure you can use in copywriting. And I read that stuff, you know, and I thought, oh, you know, this makes a lot of sense. Especially, you know, I really like Tom Hopkins because he's easy to read and he is very enthusiastic and his book How to Master the Art of Selling is really, it's just a great book. And he also talks about persistence as opposed to, I mean, you know, he tells you, you got to keep going, you got to keep going, you got to keep going, and then he tells you how to sell. And, you know, so there's a number of books like that that are, you know, create a great foundation for a copywriter. When you understand selling, then when you read John Capels and, you know, David Ogilvy and Claude Hopkins, it really creates, it all comes together so well, I think. And I at least benefited me in many ways. Cool. All right, well, all of that, those are some wonderful recommendations. You know, we have eight or 12 books that we recommend to people, but I'm definitely going to be adding Robert Ringer's books as well as these others that you've just mentioned. Yeah, I know that in addition to the successes that you've had among traditional direct response writers that I work with, or companies that I work with, you've also taken on some small local companies. And I'd like to hear more about that because a lot of our younger copywriters, for a lot of them, working for smaller local companies to begin with is a great way to get started. Right. Oh, yeah. And there's so many opportunities in those kinds of businesses because they're doing almost nothing right. So even the most mediocre copy, the most mediocre marketing ideas can sometimes work gangbusters and you can really build your copyrighting and marketing chops with those kinds of companies. The big problem though is so many of these guys have complete tunnel vision and they've never experienced direct response. They've always done everything, you know, the hard way, cold calling, running the yellow pages ads, you know, tombstone ads in the newspaper, which is what, you know, the yellow page guys and the newspaper guys, they want you to run tombstone ads. They don't want anybody to stand out. And, you know, they pose themselves as experts and these business guys, you know, believe it. And so you confront guys who just don't believe long copy works, you know, think that everything has to be short and catchy and entertaining because that's what they saw in the Super Bowl. And that's one of the big problems because they're not immersed in, you know, their ads having to generate a response. You know, usually they don't even know if they're at a generator response. They have no idea whether it's working. You know, they're working in spite of themselves or they're working just because they've been there a long time or because they got a good location or they just got momentum. A lot of companies just have momentum from years of being out there and their advertising is completely worthless. And in fact, I'll give you an excellent example of that. This is actually a friend of mine who's a builder, a very successful builder, has made a lot of money in that business. And he was never a client of mine, but one day I just handed him a tape I'd done in an interview, an interview years ago with Bob Surling, another savvy guru. And I mentioned on there the idea of testing your advertising. Don't just advertise and continue advertising without checking if it's working. Figure out a way to, you know, the whole, you know, track your results, all the basics of direct response. So this guy is kind of a contrarian. That's how he made all his money. He bought property when everyone else was selling. And he thought, gosh, that makes a lot of sense. So he didn't even test. He just canceled all his advertising, which I don't recommend, by the way. But that's what he did and he could afford it. Stopped all his billboards, stopped all his newspaper ads. He was a tracked builder, so he did quite a bit of advertising. And he was spending, I forget exactly, but somewhere in the high six figures every year, you know, $600,000, $700,000, $800,000, canceled all his advertising and he sold exactly the same number of houses. Nothing changed. So his advertising was achieving exactly nothing for him. And so he automatically saved that kind of money. And a lot of businessmen have no idea that's going on. And so they need to be aware of that. Well, let's just jump in there for a second, because there are two things that went through my mind as you were saying that. The first is a theme that I want to draw you out on a little bit later. And that has to do with accountability and advertising. Probably, I don't know the exact number, but my guess is probably 80% of the money that's spent advertising today is spent in media and in campaigns in which there's no way to know if the advertising was helpful or not. And your friend is the shining example of that, because he had no way to track the results of his advertising. He was wasting over the period of many years, millions of dollars, that came right off his bottom line on ineffective advertising. The second thing is, especially among local business people, think about it for a moment. You work hard out of your life. You finally get a nest egg together and you achieve your dream of opening your own antique store on Main Street in a quaint little tourist town someplace. And you settle in, you get the store fully stocked, you open the doors and you sit there and nobody walks through the door. And after a couple hours, if that's somebody, you know, the door opens and someone walks through. And instead of a customer, it's a representative of the local newspaper. He wants you to buy an ad. And you talk to him and you figure, well, okay, maybe so. And you buy an ad and he leaves. And a couple hours later, the door opens again, another guy walks in and it's the representative of the local cable television channel. And he wants to sell you ads. And a couple hours later, the local Yellow Page person comes in. And a couple of hours later, the count reps from the local TV station, radio station show up, a billboard agency salesman comes by. And in each case, you tell them, well, you know, I don't have an ad. And in each case when the salesman says, well, don't worry, I'll create one for you. But now you've got a salesman for the medium that you're spending money in and you're spending no experience and no track record creating ads for you. And as a result, thousands of people across this country who have achieved their dream and actually started their own businesses are sitting in their stores day by day watching their nest egg dwindle. And every one of the campaigns that they're sold are campaigns that are not trackable. So like your friend, they're terrified of canceling their advertising thinking that that would erode the whatever sales they are making. And all of this ties into this incredible scam that I want to draw you out on a little bit later, the Madison Avenue scam. Okay. Yeah, the small businessman I encounter, I mean, it's almost tragic that they pour everything into these businesses and then their advertising is lousy. They wind up growing out of business and they always blame the advertising. But they, you know, advertising doesn't work. Brackmail doesn't work. But they never really did it right. They did it based, like you said, on the advice of people with no track record, people who have no stand, and more importantly, people who really in many ways don't have any stake in your success. And when you hire someone like me or you or another copywriter, you can make the deal so that they have a stake in your success. And that's the second problem I wanted to get to with businessmen is a lot of them are really averse to doing that. They'll happily pay a salesman a commission for every sale he makes. But when a copywriter comes in the door or a marketing consultant says, hey, I want 10, 15, 25% of every new sale I bring you, they blanch. They don't want to do it. It's never been done that way. It's unheard of. And so many of them, that's one reason I think copywriters never really made huge inroads in general into these markets. I mean, there are obviously some exceptions. But they don't want to give up a piece of the action. And because they don't really consider copywriters to be salesmen, which is really the critical thing, that's all we are is salesmen. We just do it in print instead of in person. And everyone thinks, oh, yeah, I pay my secretary $50 to write a letter. So you're a copywriter, I'll pay you $100 because they don't understand that it's such a, you know, that selling in print is such a challenge and it's worth a lot of money if you do it right. So any businessman who's not willing to give me $25,000 if I hand him $100,000 is being extremely short-sighted and foolish. And so I would urge any businessman listening to this to be ready and willing to give up a piece of the action if someone's going to bring a lot of money to your bottom line. But let's get specifically to my client. I lucked out with this client mostly because he was what was softened up by Gary Halbert. He somehow became a subscriber to Gary Halbert's newsletter, which was all about the same principles we're talking about, and really liked it, really enjoyed it, and really bought into it. And so he called up Gary and fortunately for me, Gary recommended he call me. And so he gave me a call and he was sold on long copies, sold on really selling in print, and so he went at it and what he did, he wanted to get into the business of manufacturing pool cues. He had been successful owning billiard stores and pool stores and that kind of thing. And now he thought, gosh, I want to be a manufacturer. And so he started out with just a line of cues and what they all they were were cues that were low priced but high quality. Now everyone's heard that pitch before. Oh, yeah, it's high quality but it's dirt cheap, which most people, I mean, obviously is going to generate a great deal of skepticism on the part of the buyer. You know, yeah, yeah, yeah, I've heard it a thousand times before and I buy it and it's a piece of crap. And that's just a common story. So I knew right off the bat this was not, I couldn't just run a big picture of a cue and say, you know, low price is high quality. So on the very first day that we did for them, I was actually working with another writer, Gary Wolterscheid, and we together wrote this ad. It was very early in both of our careers actually. And it was probably one of the most successful ads either of us ever wrote. And it was literally a two page double truck ad in one of the pool magazines selling these new line of cues. And we pulled out every fact to prove our case that these cues were really good despite the fact that they were less expensive than other cues. And we addressed the skepticism right up front. In fact, as I recall the headline, we sort of had a big pre-head that said, choose this good at prices this low and then our main headline was simply a question, who will believe us? And that's what we addressed in the ad. Who's going to believe? Who could put out cues cheap that actually are high quality? So I think right there we hit, you know, where a lot of the customers were going to be and a lot of the owners of the stores that have to stock the cues were thinking, yeah, right. I like what Gary Benzavenga always says. That's the first reaction of anybody when they read your ad is yes, sure, they just don't believe it. So we set out to prove it with long copy and it worked like gangbusters. It was a very simple ad. Here's six reasons these cues are so great. And the cue literally took off within a year or something, I don't recall exactly, he was the number one cue in his niche. And this is the guy that started with nothing. He had no other cues. I mean he just started with nothing and had one little trade show booth. And within a few years he had his trade show booth had grown to the equivalent of 20 or 30 booths. Everything was beautiful in his booth, the nicest booth in the trade show. And I can't take credit for all that obviously, because he introduced other lines of cues. But every time we did something new we did the same thing. We launched it with long copy that explained everything and got people interested in the cue. And I'll give you one other example from that same client. He introduced a high-tech cue, a cue that was more accurate than other cues and more powerful than other cues. And you think of pool cue, it's a piece of wood. How can it be more powerful? How can it be more accurate? Well, we gave all the reasons why it was and why when you hit the ball it felt better. And at one point we literally had to stop running the ads because he couldn't get the cues. We sold out everything he had in the factory, couldn't make them fast enough, which was really kind of a nice thing. I love being able to say that. I wrote an ad that was so good they couldn't keep up with the orders. And so that worked out very well. And unfortunately, the disease of entertainment always creeps in. And that cue line that I just mentioned actually was owned by somebody else and the guy never bought into the long copy thing and kept wanting, even though there was so much high-tech, finally he dropped out and my client stopped distributing his cues. And then eventually my client's daughter took over his business and fell under the evil, influence of a graphic designer, an award-winning graphic designer who apparently won all kinds of awards for packaged goods and her stuff is beautiful. She has beautiful work. And so now, and they don't listen to anything I say, I've argued with them until I'm blue in the face. And all our ads now, which I really don't hardly have any input on anymore, are full-color, reverse type. You can't read them. I call it funky fonts. They just be huge pictures of the cues and a fancy expression or two. And it's an example of the company's doing fine because of momentum. They're an established presence now. They have this giant trade shoe boot that can show off their cues. And I always try to get through the ideas. It doesn't matter how well you're doing. It's how well could you do? What if my style of advertising could increase your sales by 20, 30, 40, 50%? Wouldn't it be worthwhile? And a lot of guys just say, you build your business on the direct response principles, you get momentum, and then suddenly the disease of the image creeps in. You know, I want to look good. I want to be respected in my community or I want, you know, whatever it is. You know, because people look at long copy ads and they'll tell you until they're blue in the face, they don't work. I'm sure Clayton, you've had the experience of showing someone one of your Magalogs that made you personally a million dollars or more, and someone will say, oh, come on, no one responds to that stuff. You know what happens all the time? Absolutely. Yeah, so that brings me to another point. I was just reading an article in USA Today from a few weeks ago where they were, what they do is they pull consumers now on whether an ad's effective or not. They don't ask them, did you buy the product? They don't judge it based on the ad, or sell any product. They just base it on, hey, did you think this was a good ad? And so they pick the most effective ads of the year in USA Today based on a poll. And so businessmen read this and go, oh, look at this, consumers say that this ad was the best ad of the year. The big mistake in that is it's not based on results, it's not based on actual sales, it's not based on profits, it's based on a poll. And if we took our ads into a bar and poll everyone, a lot of times they say, this ad is terrible, I would never read this ad. I would never buy this product. Well, they'd never read the ad, so they'd never even find out what the product is. But that's not the way you measure whether an ad is effective or not. Nobody, it doesn't matter what anyone thinks of the ad, all that matters is did it achieve results. And that's the only thing anybody should ever be looking at when they're looking at an ad. That's one of the main problems with focus groups. You get a bunch of people in the room and you show them an ad, and you do exactly what you said. And the response you get is totally meaningless because it's not, because first of all, the method you use to grab their attention was to get them in a room and say, look at this, which obviously you're not doing when the ad is actually running or being mailed or on the Internet. And then secondly, the response to the ad is not backed up with a check. And so the contrivance that totally frustrates the selling process and any input or response that gives you in that environment is suspect. And therefore those kinds of focus groups are a total waste of time. And I've also read that often focus group members will try to give the moderator the answer he or she wants. They want to please the guy in charge. Or just maybe to sound smart. Yeah, there you go. Yeah, the polls. That's the typical problem with polls. Yeah, I mean, everyone knows that nobody buys Playboy for the pictures. Everyone. When you pull Playboy readers, they all buy Playboy for the articles. And nobody buys a National Enquirer, even though it's the best selling magazine in the world. Right. The only answer that matters is one that's accompanied by a check. Yes, exactly. Exactly. And that's just a message. I mean, if every business owner just got that into their skull, they would do so much better, even if they never hired a copywriter in their life. If you just made sure you could track the results of every ad and try different things and quit listening to the so-called experts that keep telling you to build an image, build a brand. Not that there's anything wrong with having a good image or having a good brand. The problem is it's too costly for the average guy. You know, to build Miller-Wright's brand, you know, and I love the tagline, Tastes Great, Less Filling, it costs them, you know, God knows how much money they want, $100 million. You know, your average dry cleaner doesn't have $100 million to spend building a brand. He's got to, you know, rely on getting sales tomorrow, not six weeks, six months, six years from now. And so he's got to run ads that, you know, get people to bring in their laundry tomorrow, and he's got to run ads that he can track so he knows. When I read the ad in the local paper, did the guy come from that ad and bring his laundry to me? And you do that with a coupon or if nothing else, at least ask your customers, where did you hear about me? How do you know about me? And it's a lesson that, you know, has to just be driven home again and again and again to track everything religiously. And also when you, going back to the image thing again, whenever I talk to small business, but not most anybody, they rave about commercials or ads that are entertaining, they're fun, you know, everyone talks about them and such. And then inevitably if I say, so, what product were they selling? They usually have no idea what was being sold, nor did they buy the product, which is even more important. They just thought it was a great ad. And that's, again, it's just such a critical thing to forget about being entertaining. That's great to be entertaining if you can make a sale at the same time. But the sale comes first, entertainment and any other factors are purely secondary. So you can entertain, build a brand, build an image, look great in your community, and so at the same time, if I'm being hilarious, go at it. You know, more power to you, that's phenomenal. But don't let anything else take the place of sales. I know Dan Kennedy's got a great story of one of his clients who ran, you know, a long copy, boring, ugly ads that made him wealthy, but his wife always hated them. So he literally spent $100,000 just having some stuff created that looked great, that they gave him such an image to give to his wife and she could show to her friends. Just they didn't result in any sales, but it made everyone in his family feel better about his business. He wasn't just the guy running the ugly ads anymore. And so you kind of have to decide which is more important to you, making money, making a profit, staying in business, getting rich, or having your wife and your mother-in-law and whoever rave about how great your ads are and how great they look. One of the things that always cracks me up is that the same idiots who talk about how ads have to be this way or that way or be an image ad or be beautiful or be funny or be amusing or be any of these other things, the last thing they ever want to do is to test their cockamamie ideas heads up against hard-selling reason-wide benefit-oriented copy. Right. And the reason for that, well, and these are the same people who will talk about putting the customer first and making the customer king, but they don't want to be bothered with the actual numbers that the customers use to tell you whether or not they like your ads, whether or not they like your products. They don't want to know that the response rate on an ugly, long copy ad is five or six times higher than on their little image ads that they love so much. So, you know, all this stuff they say about putting the customer first is just pure baloney. The only way to put the customer first is to test different ideas, let the market tell you what it wants, and then give them that. And that's what direct response does. You and I were talking the other day about some specific ads that just crack you up that a hard-boiled, hard-core NBA who's now the CEO of a major corporation would spend $100 million putting ads that in the first place are untrackable. Okay? But in the second place, don't create a desire for the product. And in the third place, even where the concepts of the ads themselves or the video or the imaging in the ads completely dwarf the name of the product, the name of the company, and everything else. I think you mentioned Carl's Jr. Well, yeah, Carl's Jr., the hamburger chain, ran a pretty famous campaign. I can only remember one specific ad right now, and it featured Paris Hilton, one of those celebrities whose famous just for being famous, essentially making love to a hamburger as she ate it. And it was a very sensual, sexy ad that every guy I knew was like, ah, hubba hubba. And it made the rounds of the internet. I know I got it emailed to me at least once. But they weren't emailed to me like, oh, go buy a hamburger. They were really emailing it to me. Go look at Paris Hilton. And I like Paris Hilton as much as the next guy. But I certainly don't buy my hamburgers based on the fact that she's eating one, because that doesn't mean it's a good hamburger. It means nothing, and it says nothing. But it doesn't tell me what I get when I go to Carl's Jr. In any way, shape, or form, it just shows me Paris Hilton eating one. And I look at those kind of commercials and think, you know, I find them silly and ridiculous. But some people look at them and are offended by them, deeply offended, especially, you know, Christian audiences. And you can make fun of Christians and the religious right as much as you want. But the fact is they're a huge market, and they have sensibilities just like everybody else, and they want to be having a boycott of Carl's Jr. Now, I'm not averse to offending people. People are going to be offended. It's very easy to offend people. I, you know, I find political correctness to be ridiculous, and the fact that people take offense is the slightest thing to be ridiculous. But I think if you're going to offend the people, do it strategically, do it for a reason, and make sure that it actually increases your sales, it doesn't kill your sales. And, you know, I think any commercial, I know the guy, I'll give you another example in a minute. And the guy, this, well, may go into the example, everyone remembers the GoDaddy.com commercial from the Super Bowl last year that featured a Buxom woman, very pretty girl, having a wardrobe malfunction, and a lot of Janet Jackson during a Senate hearing on, again, I forget the content of the ad, but she was basically in front of a bunch of old men doing a Senate hearing, and her strap busts, and she almost loses her top, and of course all these old men are going nuts. And she's acting completely outrageous, very sexy, and everything, leaning over the microphone really far and all that jazz. And it's really, it's quite a funny commercial, quite a, you know, a rousing commercial, I suppose you could say. But, and then at the end of course they'll say GoDaddy.com, which, you know, for one thing, since you don't, GoDaddy means nothing to anybody, and that's if anybody even remembered it. And what it is, is a website that registers domain names. Cheaply, so you can go there and get a cheap domain name if you want to have an internet site. Obviously, you know, the Senate hearing thing has nothing to do with the website, and it's true what I know got consumed with this girl, understandably, but they could never have told you the name of the website. They could never have told you what the website does, or anything like that. And they spent, I don't know how many millions of dollars it cost to advertise in the Super Bowl, but it's pretty big. And there was this kind of commercial. Now the guy that I actually went on their website prior to this call started looking around on what, and the CEO has a philosophy of advertising that the reason he does it this way is all advertising has to be, as he puts it, polarizing. And I think that's a good description that Carl's Jr. ad is polarizing, and his ad is probably polarizing. People are offended, but it doesn't do you any good to polarize people if no one comes and buys your product. You know, you can piss people off all day long and have people love you and have people hate you, but you better have people buy on your product, or you're just wasting your time. So I think that's a lot, I guess my point is that's a lousy advertising philosophy. You know, instead of a philosophy, it's a tactic. And if you test polarizing, and it works for you, and it increases your sales, then fine, you know, go for it. But the thing is no one ever bothered to test it. They don't have any idea whether it really had an effect. And even if hamburger sales go up, or domain sales go up, or whatever it is, that doesn't necessarily mean it's because you're advertising. You know, you pointed out many times it could be because the weather was a certain way a certain day, because you know, there was a news story that McDonald's had food poisoning in a store in Kansas City, or you know, the Wendy's, Finger in the Chili incident. You know, all those kind of things come into play, and unless you're tracking your ads, you just don't know the real reason for the rise, or the demise of your sales. And yet the people who design those ads make millions dollars every year. And they consider it to be geniuses in the advertising world. Well, and they seduce people. They seduce small businessmen even to thinking, you know, that this is the way to advertise. Good, good, good. Well, you know, this is not a new problem. And you know, as people who has, well, let me rephrase that, as a guy that has done more to study the history of advertising and marketing than anyone I've ever met, you know, you and I have talked often about lessons that were learned a hundred years ago, and that books have been written about by some of the founders of today's advertising industry illustrating exactly the point that you're making now. Well, I'll give you a great example of that. You know, you're right about the advertising history thing. I was a history major in college. I've always been fascinated by history. I got into advertising, you know, and learned about Claude Hopkins and read his books. I just found the subject, you know, interesting and fascinating. And so I sought out other, you know, older books, and there's a fantastic history of advertising called The Mearmakers by Stephen Fox. It is just phenomenal. It goes through the history from, you know, the late 1800s up to the present day. Well, up to when the book was written in the 80s. And it just, to me, it was just fascinating. And I went and saw these characters. I looked up and I did a little more research on if I found them interesting. And, you know, most people listening to this call are familiar with the concept of salesmanship in print. It's, you know, it was devised by John E. Kennedy, you know, in the late 1800s, early 1900s. The dates escape me right now. But he shared it with Albert Lasker, the owner of Lord and Thomas. And Lasker bought into it hook, line, and sinker. He'd been on a personal quest to try to figure out what advertising was. And a lot of people thought advertising was just news. He just put the news, the newsier product, and that was it. And people had success with that back at the time. But Kennedy had the idea, no, no, no, it's selling. It's just selling. It's nothing more complicated than that. Lasker bought into it and built the most successful agency, probably in history, personally made. You know, I want to do the exact figure, but it was something like $50 million. And he was dead, you know, by 1940. So he made all this money when $50 million was real money. It was, you know, just a staggering amount of money. He took and built clients. Him and Claude Hopkins worked together and, you know, just made their clients, you know, hundreds of millions of dollars using principles that still work today. But even at the time, there were other schools of advertising. And the best example I found was a school just like today. There was a school of advertising that said advertising is a news, advertising isn't selling, advertising is entertainment. And, you know, I bet if you took a poll, that's what most people would say today. And the most famous, there was a famous advocate at this point of view, it wasn't just, his name was Ernest Elmo Calkins. And he was the big advocate, you know, that Salesmanship Apprent didn't work. It was really all about entertainment. And he worked, one of his clients was a serial company called Force Serial. And someone, it wasn't Calkins, but someone else had created a character named Sunny Jim. You know, probably that time is equivalent of the Jolly Green Giant or, you know, the Pillsbury Doboy. But his name was Sunny Jim. And at the time there was no TV. So they were building this business, you know, in print. And Sunny Jim was one of the most famous characters in America. They took polls and literally he was apparently as famous as President Roosevelt, Teddy Roosevelt, and as famous as J.P. Morgan, who was, you know, the famous banker. Everybody knew who Sunny Jim was. And they did entertaining cartoons about Sunny Jim. They did jingles about Sunny Jim. And all kinds of things that were just, you know, basically had people laughing, had people talking about it. But there was just a slight little problem. And that was that nobody was buying the serial. They were all laughing at Sunny Jim. You know, everyone was loving Sunny Jim, but no one bought the serial. And finally, the company threw in the towel and hired that famous advocate of salesmanship in print, Johnny Kennedy, to write a new advertising campaign for them. Unfortunately, as far as I know, that campaign has lost to history. Well, maybe somebody's dug it up somewhere. I know there's a lot of Hopkins ads available on the web, but I never was really able to find much from Kennedy. And he wrote a series of ads for Forrest Serial using his principles of salesmanship in print. And, you know, the next thing you know, Forrest Serial is selling like gangbusters. And Colkins himself, I've got a quote here I looked up before we got on the phone. And as he said after he was humbled by Kennedy, he said, the advertising absolutely sold Sunny Jim to the public, but it did not sell Forrest. And that's the key point. And the best, absolute best modern example of that, and it's been a few years now, hopefully people remember this, was a campaign that Nissan ran a few years back that was designed by one of those hot, hot creative agencies, you know, the geniuses. It was an agency, it used to be shy at day. I think now they had through mergers that we became TWA, shy at day or something along those lines. And they created an ad campaign featuring action figures, you know, Ken and Barbie and GI Joe type figures driving the cars, they had a dinosaur grab one of the characters and drop them into a car. And it's just really entertaining. I loved these commercials. I thought they were hysterical. There was one I recall where a seagull was chasing a brand new Nissan through the streets trying to soil it. And the guy just barely made it into his garage and the seagull wound up impaled on his garage door or something like that. Really funny stuff, really hilarious. And the advertising industry went nuts for this stuff. It was called the Wall Street Journal, literally called it the most successful TV commercial of the year. One of the commercials, I don't recall specifically which one. You know, magazines were calling it the best ad of the year. The guy, the copywriters and art directors were literally on opera discussing how brilliant they were and they were going to make a TV series about these characters and the ads. And you know, and they, when, what's his name, the president or the creative director for TWA, shy at day entered the advertising all of fame. And when he did, when they inducted him, they played the music from the commercial. I don't recall what it was. I think it might have been a Rolling Stones song or something. And as soon as they started the music and he stood up, the audience gave him a standing ovation. And they were going, that's for this guy. But all this, you know, fame and fortune for these guys, but there was just again one little problem. Sales for Nissan were dropping like a rock. They were literally plummeting and the car dealers were in revolt against Nissan. And in fact, I got these statistics that in the first month of the campaign sales fell 2.7% over the previous year. The next month they fell 10.2%. Then they could fell 4%. Then they fell 2% again. So their sales are slowly falling. And meanwhile, who's the big competitor? Toyota. And their sales are booming on the backs of, you know, crappy, boring, benefit-oriented rebate ads. You know, buy a Nissan today and get a $1,000 rebate. It was still on the cards, even though, you know, you'd never win an award for that ad. No one would ever put you on Oprah for that ad. But the thing is, the dealers don't want to be on Oprah. They need to make a living. They got to feed their families. So what happened was, the dealers went bananas and finally the Wall Street Journal had this incredible quote that I think sums up advertising agencies. And when he was told about their complaints, he said, that's car dealers. They're forever bitching about something. There are always people that like to damn things that are new. And I, the arrogance of that kind of a statement, to me, is mind-boggling. But it's also a lesson for every businessman and every corporation in America that the attitude of an ad agency is more, we want accolades, we want awards, your sales, be damned. And if you don't like it, screw you. And that's basically all he was saying. Eventually, however, Nissan executives loved all the attention, but, you know, when sales are dropping, something's got to be changed. And so eventually, they went back to the old boring lame, horrible ads that everyone hates. And within a month, sales literally increased 10% over the previous year. And the next month, they increased 15% over the previous year. So whenever you're tempted to entertain, you know, be darn sure you're ready to absorb a big drop in sales. And whenever someone tries to tell you it's got to be entertaining, demand that they show you actual results. You know, not, oh, sales increased. You know, sales increased by how much? Give me specifics. How many more units did you actually sell? Absolutely essential. Yeah, and I love, there's another quote from the Wall Street Journal that I got out of this long article that was interesting. And it said, not long ago, the conventional wisdom on Madison Avenue held that advertising was all about giving people a compelling reason to buy a product. TWBA Chired Day believed advertising could have a different goal, to create flashy images for a client and turn the company name, company's name, into a household name. And when you think about it, that the conventional wisdom used to be give people a reason to buy the product? That's just insanity. You damn well better give people a reason to buy your product or they're not going to buy it. And just having a flashy image does not, I won't say never, makes a person buy your product. You never say never, but in general, 99% of the time, especially for the people listening to this call, building a flashy image is a complete and total waste of time that will cost you money. And you darn well better give people a reason to buy your product. So let me have one caveat to it. As I said before, it doesn't mean you can't use humor. It doesn't mean you can't be entertaining. It doesn't mean any of that. You know, if you can make me laugh my ass off and still get me to buy your product, great, go for it. Do it. But it's exceedingly difficult and you've got to test. You've got to make sure that it's actually entertaining me and selling your product. That's really the essential point. That's all that matters. And track your ads, figure out if they're working, and quit following for this philosophy. You see a spouse by all the ad agencies whose results are never, ever tracked and they will never, ever want you to track them. And they will stop you from tracking them. And the same goes for your media reps, your Yellow Pages, your Newspaper ads. They don't want you to track because what if their medium sucks for your business? And they also don't want anybody to stand out. They like the idea of everyone having the same looking ad. So if you flip open the Yellow Pages, you're looking for a dentist. Every ad is Joshmo DDS. How do you pick a dentist based on that? Whereas, you know, the one guy that has an ad, you know, ten mistakes to avoid when choosing a dentist, you're going to look at his ad and go, hey, you know, I better read this before I just pick some random dentist. And you read the ten reasons and naturally at the end of the thing it says, you know, for more information call my free recorded message or send away from my free booklet on how to, you know, have your teeth whiter than ever, cleaner than ever, you know, whatever it is. And that gets people, you know, gives people information they need to make a decision. And it makes you, and you want to stand out, that will make you stand out even though it's a long copy boring, you know, six reasons to, or six ways to, six mistakes to avoid when picking a dentist. You know, but people want that kind of information. And you've got to test, obviously, you know, maybe six mistakes isn't the right approach, but there's some approach that is the right approach. Even more importantly, if you tell them to request a report or you tell them to call a free recorded number, you can track it. Most dentists won't even ask you where you got their name, you know, and this way you can track it, you don't even have to ask that question, you know. This guy requested a free report, he requested it from, you know, GTE Yellow Pages or whatever it is. And then you know, are these Yellow Pages working for me? And, you know, that's true. There's no other advertising I would ever recommend doing for any small businessman. So, you know, we got off on the tangent there, we're actually talking a little bit about the history. Let me go back to that for a minute. Like you said, most of the mistakes that have been made in advertising today, they were made 100 years ago. And Claude Hopkins, it all goes back to Claude Hopkins. Now, Johnny Kennedy wrote some good stuff, and I think it's available here and there, but really Claude Hopkins is the epitome of salesmanship and print. He pulled it all together into easily found books called Scientific Advertising and My Life in Advertising, which usually I see sold as one book. And it's all there. And there are certain things in there where Hopkins was wrong or he's dated, but the philosophy, the principles, everything behind it is right there for any businessman to read. And if you did nothing else but read those two books and followed the advice, obviously, you would find your business exploding by leaps and bounds compared to your competitors who were ignoring all this advice. And it's all about the things, I mean, it's not like we're preaching anything new in terms of benefits and unique selling proposition and sell benefits instead of features, give people a reason why this is not new stuff. This has been around since we were gleams in our daddy's eyes. And the guys who wrote this stuff were dead before we were born, but they're just ignored, I think, because everyone always wants the next big thing. What's next? They want a new idea, and it's a natural human impulse that should always be taken advantage of in your advertising. The latest secret, the latest this, the latest that, it's one thing to sell that way. It's another thing to live your life that way when the foundational principles are right there for the taking. And if you haven't applied them and you jump on the next big thing, you're wasting your time. And so, obviously, like the idea, your idea of dominant emotion. Emotion is always permeated advertising, and the greats have always advocated using emotions. And so it is a great idea, though I think you've probably made more out of it than anybody else in really focusing your ads on that. But most guys will jump on dominant emotions, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, but they still don't have the foundational principles. You know, they're not doing reason why. They're not doing benefits. There's nothing unique about their business. And they really have to, you know, do that and then add the emotional stuff. And your business is almost guaranteed to do better, you know, far better than before. Assuming, again, you've got a product people want, you've got, you know, and you've got an audience for your prospects, for whatever you're selling.
